text:
It's pretty neat how current this book reads being published in 1993. I like Nardi's counter examples of driving cars and playing music as alternatives to natural language ("mundane conversation") as the default medium of human machine communication.

Reminds me of something I read recently in <https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/18770267-make-it-stick|Make It Stick>—that there is no empirical support for the claim that instruction in one’s preferred learning style (e.g. "I'm a visual learner") improves learning outcomes. It's the subject matter that dictates the best medium, e.g. diagrams for geometry, words for poetry

text:
Similar, reminds me of how a lot of really old texts that started as stuff that was orally transmitted are filled with redundancy and repetition.

In The Information A History, a Theory, a Flood, James Gleick makes an interesting juxtaposition between repetition, information, and transmission and how they all relate to what I’d call robustness.

text: I had a professor in my undergrad who opened up the intro computer security course with an argument that cells expend a majority of their energy on self-defense  (or something like that, IANA biologist) and this was supposed to mean that we should be devoting more computational resources to security/resilience/error resistance

elements.text: I had a professor in my undergrad who opened up the intro computer security course with an argument that cells expend a majority of their energy on self-defense  (or something like that, IANA biologist) and this was supposed to mean that we should be devoting more computational resources to security/resilience/error resistance

text: I came across this viewpoint in A Third Window, which described fragile systems as those reliant on a single feedback loop for one or more of its required resources, and described resilient systems as those built of overlapping feedback loops with multiple sources of each resource, and the tendency of the former to become the latter when there’s a dearth of competition for the same resources.

elements.text: I came across this viewpoint in A Third Window, which described fragile systems as those reliant on a single feedback loop for one or more of its required resources, and described resilient systems as those built of overlapping feedback loops with multiple sources of each resource, and the tendency of the former to become the latter when there’s a dearth of competition for the same resources.

text: This topic always makes me think of this oldie but goodie: a lovely <https://youtu.be/cmByV8wQCtc?si=YHLL0cgmLiDmSiU1|talk on efficiency vs resilience in evolutionary systems>.

text: I suspect that a big reason for accessibility being neglected in computing is ignorance. People who can't participate in the Web as it is today are also invisible to those who can and do, so we don't know what exactly their issues are nor what we could do to improve the situation. It's a kind of digital divide.

elements.text: I suspect that a big reason for accessibility being neglected in computing is ignorance. People who can't participate in the Web as it is today are also invisible to those who can and do, so we don't know what exactly their issues are nor what we could do to improve the situation. It's a kind of digital divide.

text: Definitely an interesting chapter, though I struggled a bit with some of the linguistics analysis. We know now that computers are capable of mundane conversation, so I feel like there are some follow up questions worth asking

elements.text: Definitely an interesting chapter, though I struggled a bit with some of the linguistics analysis. We know now that computers are capable of mundane conversation, so I feel like there are some follow up questions worth asking

text:
Nardi seems to suggest that programs require too much precision to be described without formal semantics. But we often talk to each other about how code works (e.g. specifications aren’t written in code, but are an informal means of meta-programming).

I there agree that code is a good direct interface with the computer, but for many of the computer users who want to program but don’t know how to code, I feel that conversational model in the AI era has a lot more promise than this chapter foresaw.

text: FWIW, halfway through the last paragraph I was thinking “cool, now we’re done with the front matter, let’s get into this juicy accessibility vs adaptability thing!” – and then the post ended. So take that as a sign that, if you were to write more on this thought (or if you already have?) I am interested to read it.

text: Yeah, the chapter felt like the AI chapter with Nardi as the AI skeptic. I am craving a blog post or something with her thoughts 30 years later on how the chapter aged (looks like she hasn't blogged in over a decade though, so I'm not getting my hopes up haha).
